title: Lining a wall before papering
subtitle: Cross-line the wall to hide surface irregularities and give paper a stable ground
excerpt: How to prepare a wall for wallpaper by hanging lining paper horizontally across the wall. Cross-lining evens out minor surface irregularities, hides old filler patches, and gives the decorative paper a stable, absorbent ground to bond to.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s).

glossaryTerms.definition: A plain white or off-white paper hung on walls and ceilings before decorative paper. It provides a smooth, uniform ground for the decorative paper to bond to and prevents minor surface irregularities from showing through the finished wall.

The technique of hanging lining paper horizontally across a wall while the decorative paper hangs vertically. The joints of the two layers never align, which prevents the decorative paper from opening at a line of weakness in the plaster or previous surface.

Applying a diluted wallpaper paste or a proprietary size coat to the wall before hanging paper. Sizing reduces the suction of bare plaster, gives the paste time to dry, and allows the paper to be slid into position after hanging.

content.text: Lining paper

is a preparatory step, not a cosmetic one. On a wall that has been replastered, patched, or previously stripped, lining covers the variation in suction between bare plaster, filler, and old decoration that would otherwise make the decorative paper dry at different rates and show as patches.
Cross-lining
runs the lining paper horizontally so its joints never coincide with the vertical joints of the decorative paper above it.
What you need
Lining paper.
Grade 800 (medium weight) for most walls under standard wallpaper. Grade 1200 (heavy) for textured or heavily patched walls.
Wallpaper paste.
Heavy-duty paste mixed slightly thicker than the decorative-paper rate. The bucket-and-brush method is fine for lining; a paste table speeds the job.
Paperhanger's brush, seam roller, and a clean straight-edge or smoother for pressing seams.
Plumb line or spirit level for establishing the first horizontal guide.
Step 1, prepare the wall

Fill all holes and cracks. Sand smooth. Remove any flaking or loose paint with the scraper. If the wall was previously covered in old wallpaper, strip it completely and allow the plaster to dry for at least 3 days in a heated room.
Size the wall

by brushing on a diluted wallpaper paste (1 part paste to 4 parts water) and allowing it to dry. Sizing prevents the wall pulling moisture out of the lining paper too quickly, which causes the paper to dry before it can be smoothed flat.
Step 2, measure and cut the lining paper

Measure the width of the wall and add 50 mm for trimming at each end. Cut the first length. Measure the remaining drop (ceiling to floor minus the width of the first length) to see how many horizontal lengths the wall needs. A standard domestic wall 2.4 m high with 530 mm-wide lining paper needs 4 to 5 lengths.
Step 3, paste and hang

Apply paste to the back of the lining paper on a paste table, ensuring the edges are fully pasted. Fold the pasted length concertina-style without creasing (paper-to-paper folds) and allow to soak for the time the paste manufacturer specifies (typically 3 to 5 minutes). Start at the top of the wall, hanging the first length just below the ceiling with its top edge aligned to a horizontal pencil guide drawn from a spirit level. Smooth from the centre outward with the paperhanger's brush, removing air pockets. Butt the lower edge of each length tightly to the upper edge of the previous one without overlapping. Do not overlap lining paper joints; an overlap creates a ridge that telegraphs through the decorative paper.
Step 4, trim and allow to dry

Trim the overlap at each end with a straight-edge and a sharp utility knife while the paper is still wet. Roll the joins with a seam roller. Allow the lining paper to dry completely before hanging the decorative paper: typically 24 hours in a ventilated room. The lining paper will look slightly patchy when wet and will even out as it dries. Hanging decorative paper over wet lining causes both layers to stretch and bubble.
Common mistakes
Hanging lining paper vertically.
Vertical lining is correct when the wall will be painted, not papered. For papering, always cross-line horizontally.
Overlapping joints.
A 2 mm overlap creates a ridge visible through the finished paper. Butting the edges cleanly is the technique.
Hanging decorative paper before the lining is dry.
Both layers stretch and the decorative paper lifts or bubbles. The lining must be fully dry to the touch and flat to the wall before decorating over it.
